review_text:
My first 1 star review goes to...the very deserving Circus Circus.  This review has everything to do with their employees and nothing to do with the amenities of the hotel and casino.

We all know how fun it is to relive your childhood upstairs where all the games and circus shows go down so my friends and I spent some time up there.  We sat down to play Circus Downs, the game where you roll a ball into holes to try to get your horse across the board, but there was a cheater amongst us.  He stole a ball..and used 2 to play.  The worker did nothing to stop him and even awarded that jerk a prize at the end.  Seriously?! Little kids are playing this game and you're going to cheat them from a fair game??  When I asked the employee to let us play another round, he just ignored me!  He didn't even apologize for the cheater or simply say no.  A friend and I decided to report this situation to security...which turned out to be no help AT ALL.  

Us: *tap* Excuse me.
Security biatch: Don't touch me.
Us: I need to report an incident. We were upstairs-
Security: *with LOTS of attitude* Upstairs? Upstairs where? Midway? 
Us: Um...upstairs where all the gam..
Security: Get to the point. What.Do.You.Want?
*Oh hellll no! At this point..I've had it up to HERE with her attitude!*
Us: If you're busy and you need to be somewhere, then I can talk to someone else.  Just point me to the direction of a manager.
Security: *throws back her jacket flap to reveal her "badge* Security.
Us: Tells her the story...blah blah 
Security: What? So this is about a teddy bear? 
Us: NO, this about ethics and the way the employee inappropriately handled the situation.
Security: How old are you?
Us:......Why does that matter?
Security: I have the right to ask.

WHAT A BEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEEE!!! Really?! You're a security guard and you're going around riding on your high horse??  Are you even doing your job securing the premises? I wish I had gotten HER name so that I could report her!!

As you can guess, talking to the manager of midway was pointless.  He apologized for the "terrible predicament" we were in and how he understands "it sucks."  WHY ARE THEY TALKING TO US AS IF WE'RE 8 YEARS OLD?! 

adijgoiasjdgoiasdg CIRCUS CIRCUS YOU GUYS SUCK!!!
issue: Employee ignored cheating, allowed the cheater to win a prize, and failed to address the complaint or apologize
fixes: Deploy a “Cheating Incident” workflow in Zendesk with a mandatory 24‑hour resolution SLA that auto‑assigns any “cheat‑win” ticket to the floor manager and triggers an automatic escalation email if the case remains open past the deadline. | - Integrate a post‑interaction NPS survey via Qualtrics that includes a “Did you receive an apology?” question; set a weekly dashboard alert when the apology rate falls below 90 % so supervisors can intervene promptly. | - Schedule quarterly “Live‑Case Review” meetings where the floor manager watches recorded footage (captured by the store’s CCTV system) of every cheat detection, reviews the documented response, and logs corrective actions in a shared Google Sheet tracker. | - Launch a micro‑learning module on Axonify titled “Resolving Cheating Complaints,” requiring employees to achieve ≥95 % quiz scores before their next shift, with completion tracked in the LMS and tied to performance bonuses.
rationale: All recommendations directly address the core issue: failure to respond to cheating and lack of apology. Each specifies exact tools (Zendesk, Qualtrics, CCTV, Axonify, Google Sheets), measurable actions (SLA, quiz scores, NPS threshold), and clear triggers. Actionability is high—steps are precise. Concreteness is excellent. Feasibility is moderate (3) due to reliance on CCTV footage review, LMS integration, and cross-functional coordination for quarterly meetings, which exceeds trivial or low-effort thresholds. No point deduction for format.

review_text:
My review pertains only to the Pet Boarding facilities.

Their "Hound Hotel" sounds/looks really good online, but in person it was a real let down.  The kennel is in a room off the parking garage, steps from the city sidewalk.  I was told it was by a security desk, but when we arrived, that whole tower was closed and unattended.  If somone were able to get into the room, they could get to your dog through the space at the top of the cage, even though the cage was locked.  The cameras are a nice feature, but are you gonna watch the dog 24/7?  Even if you saw someone doing something with your dog, it would take you 10 minutes to get there from the other tower.  We packed up and went to the Reno Plaza hotel, who allows pets in the room.

If you care for your pet, you would not use these facilities.
issue: The online description of the "Hound Hotel" is misleading and does not match the actual facility experience
fixes: Deploy a cross‑functional review workflow in Asana where the marketing copywriter, product manager, and facilities lead each sign off on new “Hound Hotel” listings before publishing, ensuring all images, amenities, and pricing align with the physical space. | - Integrate a post‑stay verification step into your SurveyMonkey NPS survey by asking guests to rate “Description Accuracy” (1‑10) and automatically flag any rating < 7 for immediate content correction in the CMS. | - Create a static HTML mock‑up of the hotel page in a shared Confluence space that mirrors the live site; require every code push to pass a visual diff check against this master version before going live. | - Schedule a weekly audit using the Screaming Frog SEO spider tool to capture all URLs mentioning “Hound Hotel,” then run a Python script to compare meta‑description keywords with onsite amenity data and generate a report for corrective actions within two business days.
rationale: All recommendations directly address the misalignment between online description and physical experience. Each specifies concrete tools (Asana, SurveyMonkey, Confluence, Screaming Frog, Python) and measurable actions. Steps are clear and executable. However, feasibility is capped at 3 due to the need for a custom Python script and integration across multiple systems, requiring moderate coordination and development effort—beyond trivial or low-effort implementation.

Concreteness is 5: All tools and systems are named specifically. Actionability is 4: Steps are clear but assume existing infrastructure and training, which isn't confirmed.

review_text:
Ahhh Circus Circus Reno, lets take a tour shall we?  Keep in mind we're not expecting much, this IS Reno, not Vegas.

Lets check in!  Go to the front desk and be greeted by the most somber and unpleasant reception desk staff I've encountered.  Pretty much everybody we talk to up there seem to hate their life and made sure to show it.  What a great vibe to start our trip!

Take a walk through the casino floor on the way to the room.  Take a look around.  The wait staff looks like they've been working there since 1975, jaw-jacked clientele super focused on their slot game nearing the end of their 72 hour meth bender (bet your ass they're staying in the room next to you!).  Outstanding!  Unless you're waiting for the tram to take you to the Sky Tower (which "sometimes" works), looks like you're walking about 5-10 minutes to your room carrying all your shit, probably should have just got a bellhop, but expect the same bad customer service, so we'll do it ourselves.

Arriving in the room, you'll be greeted by questionable bedding and furniture that looks like it's been sitting in the room since about the time that the wait staff was hired (circa 1975 or so... at least the TV was from the late 80s or early 90s).  You might even be lucky enough to find some dirty towels in the bathroom.  If you forgot your lighter in the car you're shit outta luck, since there's no matches to be found in the room.

Spend about 60 minutes downstairs and get stared at by creepy crackheads and ignored by wait staff.  Lets go get a drink at the bar!  Why does the bartender act like I'm unconvincing him be even being there?  There's no "Hey bud what can I get cha" here, just a blank stare and a nod acknowledging that you did, in fact, place an order.

...


Word to the wise:  Go ahead and stay at any of the other Casinos apart from the Circus Circus-Silver Legacy-El Dorado group.  I'd recommend paying a little extra and staying at the Peppermill or Atlantis (Reno's best "Vegas-like" experience), or if you have to stay downtown, go ahead and stay at the Sands Regency, at least the staff is more friendly and the rooms are bigger, for a cheaper price.  Clientele is slightly on the shady side as well, but it's Reno, I wouldn't expect anything more.
issue: Reception and front‑desk staff were unfriendly and displayed a negative attitude
fixes: Deploy a real‑time sentiment monitoring dashboard (e.g., Medallia) on reception screens that flags any guest rating ≤3 stars within 24 hours and automatically routes the alert to the floor manager for immediate coaching. | - Institute a mandatory “Daily Attitude Pulse” survey sent via SurveyMonkey at the end of every shift; set a KPI that ≥90 % of respondents rate staff friendliness ≥4/5, with weekly compliance reports reviewed in the team huddle. | - Integrate a structured microlearning module in Lessonly titled “Positive Greetings & Active Listening,” requiring each front‑desk associate to complete a 10‑minute scenario video followed by a 5‑question quiz before their next scheduled shift. | - Schedule quarterly mystery‑shopper visits through Market Force, assigning a scorecard focused on greeting tone and body language; tie 15 % of quarterly bonus eligibility to achieving a minimum average score of 85 %. | - Launch a peer‑shadowing program where one receptionist observes another for a 30‑minute block each week, documented in a shared Google Sheet, and require a brief reflection note on how the observed behavior could be replicated.
rationale: All recommendations directly address the theme of unfriendly front-desk behavior with specific tools (Medallia, SurveyMonkey, Lessonly, Market Force, Google Sheets) and measurable actions. Each is actionable and concrete. However, feasibility is capped at 3 due to the reliance on external vendors (Market Force), mandatory training modules with time constraints, and the need for cross-team coordination—moderate effort, not trivial.

Arriving in the room, you'll be greeted by questionable bedding and furniture that looks like it's been sitting in the room since about the time that the wait staff was hired (circa 1975 or so... at least the TV was from the late 80s or early 90s).  You might even be lucky enough to find some dirty towels in the bathroom.  If you forgot your lighter in the car you're shit outta luck, since there's no matches to be found in the room.

Spend about 60 minutes downstairs and get stared at by creepy crackheads and ignored by wait staff.  Lets go get a drink at the bar!  Why does the bartender act like I'm unconvincing him be even being there?  There's no "Hey bud what can I get cha" here, just a blank stare and a nod acknowledging that you did, in fact, place an order.

...


Word to the wise:  Go ahead and stay at any of the other Casinos apart from the Circus Circus-Silver Legacy-El Dorado group.  I'd recommend paying a little extra and staying at the Peppermill or Atlantis (Reno's best "Vegas-like" experience), or if you have to stay downtown, go ahead and stay at the Sands Regency, at least the staff is more friendly and the rooms are bigger, for a cheaper price.  Clientele is slightly on the shady side as well, but it's Reno, I wouldn't expect anything more.
issue: Room furnishings were outdated, bedding and towels were dirty, and basic amenities like matches were missing
fixes: Deploy a digital housekeeping checklist in iAuditor (or a similar mobile audit app) that requires staff to photograph every bedding item, towel set, and amenity before room release; non‑compliant photos trigger automatic alerts to supervisors. | - Integrate RFID tags into all linens and towels, linking them to the property management system so any tag reading “dirty” during inventory automatically generates a work order for immediate replacement. | - Schedule weekly deep‑cleaning rotations using the Housekeeping Operations Dashboard in Opera PMS, assigning specific rooms to specific teams and tracking completion rates against a 95 % on‑time KPI. | - Install QR‑code stickers on bedside tables that guests scan with their smartphones to request missing items (matches, toothbrushes, etc.), routing requests instantly to the front desk via Zingle’s guest messaging platform. | - Replace dated furniture and décor with modular, low‑maintenance pieces from IKEA Business Solutions, and set a quarterly refresh schedule to keep the visual standard consistent across all rooms.
rationale: All recommendations directly address room cleanliness issues with specific tools and measurable actions. Each is highly actionable and concrete, naming real systems (iAuditor, Opera PMS, Zingle, RFID, IKEA). However, feasibility is rated 2 due to high effort: RFID integration, QR-code deployment, and furniture overhaul require significant capital, vendor coordination, and operational restructuring—none are trivial or low-effort. The cost and rollout complexity disqualify them from higher feasibility scores.

review_text: Raquel, Brittney, & some middle eastern dude that works in the VIP downstairs....all terrible customer service. As a VIP member I should be receiving better care. They were all rude and unhelpful. I will never stay here again. I can not believe this service. It takes over an hour after talking to 4 different people and I still don't have a wifi connection after I paid for it? Not okay.
issue: Rude and unhelpful staff providing poor customer service, especially for VIP members
fixes: Deploy a real‑time sentiment dashboard in Zendesk (or Freshdesk) that flags any ticket with a negative CSAT score below 80% for immediate review by a senior manager. | - Launch a mandatory 45‑minute microlearning module on VIP etiquette using Axonify, requiring agents to achieve ≥90% quiz scores before they handle VIP cases again. | - Implement a weekly “VIP Service Pulse” meeting where a cross‑functional team reviews the top five VIP complaints from the past week and assigns corrective actions tracked in Jira. | - Integrate a post‑interaction NPS survey via Qualtrics specifically targeting VIP customers, linking responses to individual agent performance dashboards in Power BI for quarterly bonus adjustments.
rationale: All recommendations directly address the theme of poor VIP service and rude staff. Each is specific, uses real tools (Zendesk, Axonify, Jira, Qualtrics, Power BI), and includes measurable actions. Feasibility is scored 3 because while tools exist, integrating NPS to agent dashboards for bonuses and requiring quiz scores before handling VIP cases implies new policy enforcement and tracking—moderate effort requiring cross-team coordination and potential HR alignment, not trivial.

review_text:
I work in Reno Feb-March, for the last ....years, beds have not been upgraded for many years, the air quality is very bad, especially for one whose profession is a vocalist.  haven't tried the food here,  my room had a broken heater and had to be replaced.  All in all, Would never stay here, but it's provided by my employer, so that's the catch.  don't know how Reno will sustain these large hotel casinos with such poor entertainment.

I'm on the road about 6 months a year throughout North America and Europe, and about 95% of hotels have free wifi.  Circus Circus charges $10.00 per day for wifi, an added out of pocket expense for business travellers.  For one who must make many international calls, free wifi is a must for making travel reservations.  The only hotels charging for wifi these days are the big budget hotels, or downtown Reno.  Just Sayin', time to change Circus Circus.   Not happy paying for premium wireless, when I can't log on to internet banking or try to watch a news video of a dangerous high wind weather alert.  Just a note to all of you who like comfortable new beds,  I would go with El Dorado.  They have new flat screen TVs, but Circus Circus still has the 1990 model.  You'd think with all of that gambling revenue, they could upgrade some important things.  Hotel premium wireless is $13.00 per day,  download speeds are .31 MBPS,  You might be able to watch a 30 second video in 3 hours at that rate.  Unacceptable.  
On a positive note, some good live music in the Lounge.
issue: Beds are outdated and uncomfortable, not upgraded for years.
fixes: Launch a **quarterly mattress replacement cycle** tracked in your ERP (e.g., SAP) with a KPI of ≤ 10% of beds older than 36 months; automatically generate purchase orders when the age threshold is reached. | - Deploy a **digital comfort survey** via Qualtrics on checkout and after stay, targeting a ≥ 90% satisfaction rating before any bed upgrade decision is made. | - Introduce a **vendor‑managed inventory (VMI) program** with SealedAir for mattress liners and pillows, ensuring real‑time stock levels and automatic replenishment when inventory falls below a 15‑day safety buffer. | - Install **IoT pressure sensors** (e.g., Tempur-Pedic SmartBase) on all beds to capture usage patterns and trigger alerts when wear metrics exceed preset thresholds, enabling predictive maintenance scheduling.
rationale: All recommendations are highly relevant to bed comfort and directly address the outdated mattress issue. Actionability is strong but slightly hindered by the need for interpretation (e.g., 'preset thresholds' in IoT sensors). Concreteness is excellent with specific tools (SAP, Qualtrics, SealedAir, Tempur-Pedic SmartBase). Feasibility is low (2) due to high cost and complexity of IoT sensors and VMI integration, which require significant capital, vendor negotiation, and system integration—far beyond trivial or low-effort implementation.

review_text:
I give it 2 stars only because it was so darn cheap.  I stayed there for 30 a night. I mean c'mon.  It really doesn't get cheaper than that - even regular motels are more expensive. 

The games there aren't what they used to be when I went as a child and they're so much more pricey.  It's fun to play them again though and win those useless prizes. I actually had fun playing the arcade games where you win tickets and trade them in though.  They still have the old school games that I don't even remember the names of because they're that old.   The shows were so half assed but I wasn't expecting an extravagant show anyway.  It's sad because reno is so empty unless there's an event going on.  The food in that vicinity wasn't that great either and I noticed while and after eating that everyone that was there was 60+.  It's a great meat market for the seniors! haha
issue: The motel is extremely cheap, suggesting low quality and poor value.
fixes: Deploy a quarterly “Room Quality Audit” using a standardized checklist in Trello (or Asana) with photos uploaded via Google Photos; any score < 8/10 triggers a corrective work order within 48 hours. | - Introduce tiered pricing through the property management system (e.g., Opera PMS) by adding a “Premium” rate bundle that includes upgraded linens, toiletries, and Wi‑Fi, and track uptake via daily sales reports in Power BI. | - Launch a post‑stay NPS survey via Qualtrics that asks guests to rate value vs. price on a 0‑10 scale; set a target average ≥ 7.5 and automatically generate follow‑up emails for scores ≤ 6 to offer complimentary upgrades or discounts. | - Partner with local restaurants and attractions to create bundled “Stay & Dine” packages sold through the booking engine (e.g., SiteMinder); monitor package conversion rates weekly and adjust pricing based on cost‑plus margin calculations in Excel.
rationale: All recommendations directly address the pricing-value perception issue by linking quality signals to pricing tiers. Each is specific, uses real tools (Trello, Opera PMS, Qualtrics, SiteMinder, Power BI, Excel), and includes measurable actions. Feasibility is 3 due to requiring cross-system integration (e.g., linking Qualtrics to email automation, SiteMinder bundling), which exceeds trivial or low-effort thresholds, but remains within moderate effort with existing tools.

fixes: Deploy a cloud‑based arcade management platform (e.g., PlayCRAZY) to remotely update firmware on all machines within 48 hours of new game releases, ensuring every title stays current without hardware upgrades. | - Introduce a tiered pricing model using Square’s “Discounted Bundle” feature that offers a 15 % discount when customers purchase three credits at once, automatically applied at checkout via QR code scanners. | - Replace low‑margin titles with high‑ROI VR/AR experiences sourced from Unity Asset Store, installing a single VR headset per location and tracking usage through Google Analytics Events to justify ROI within six months. | - Launch a monthly “Game Rotation” calendar in Trello, assigning a staff member to swap out two games per shift; monitor attendance data in Tableau dashboards to correlate increases in average spend per guest with the rotation schedule.
rationale: All recommendations directly address outdated games and overpricing with specific tools (PlayCRAZY, Square, Unity Asset Store, Trello, Google Analytics, Tableau). Each is actionable and concrete. However, feasibility is rated 2 due to high effort: deploying a new cloud platform (PlayCRAZY) requires integration, licensing, and training; VR/AR deployment demands hardware, content licensing, and staff retraining; and cross-tool integration (Trello, Tableau, Google Analytics) requires significant setup. These are not trivial or low-effort fixes.

I give it 2 stars only because it was so darn cheap.  I stayed there for 30 a night. I mean c'mon.  It really doesn't get cheaper than that - even regular motels are more expensive.

review_text:
We made reservations just a day prior and then arrive only to be told that all circus performance acts are off until they finish construction of a new stage (which is due to open in Nov). This issue here is they made no mention of this on their webpage. When I complained they offered a full refund on the spot, but after driving all day we were not in the mood to shop around for another hotel.
Also, it was apparent the bedding either wasn't washed or not washed enough.
issue: Circus performance was cancelled without prior notice on the website, leading to disappointment.
fixes: Deploy a real‑time event‑status widget on the website (e.g., built with React + Node.js) that pulls data from your ticketing API (e.g., Eventbrite) and displays “Cancelled” or “Pending” status instantly, preventing false listings. | - Configure automated SMS/email notifications via Twilio/SendGrid triggered by the same API webhook when any show’s status changes to “cancelled,” ensuring customers receive notice within 15 minutes of the change. | - Integrate a Slack integration with the ticketing system so the operations team receives an instant alert whenever a cancellation occurs, prompting them to update all external channels (website, social media, etc.) before the next scheduled check‑in. | - Set up a post‑cancel recovery workflow in HubSpot that sends a personalized apology email with a 10 % coupon code and logs the incident for KPI tracking, aiming for ≤ 24‑hour resolution time.
rationale: The response is perfectly relevant, actionable, and concrete, naming specific tools (React, Node.js, Twilio, SendGrid, Slack, HubSpot) and measurable outcomes. However, feasibility is scored 2 because deploying a real-time widget, integrating multiple systems, and building a post-cancel workflow requires significant engineering effort, cross-team coordination, and likely new software integration—far beyond trivial or low-effort changes. This exceeds the threshold for a 4 or 5 in feasibility under the strict rubric.

review_text:
This review is on the kids Midway game area:

As a child, I lived for Circus Circus! My family would come here several times a year and it was amazing and fun.

I took my 9 year old son here this past weekend for the first time and it was garbage to how it was before. They did not at all upgrade this place. They even took out one of the popular games Fantasia! The shows they had going on werent that entertaining. The games were getting overpriced and there wasn't really any new and fun exciting games. My son basically got bored in an hour and really wasn't that entertained by everything. I had to agree with him big time because I knew it was trash compared to what it was before. They need to do a huge upgrade to this place! I'm sure if they did, they would get a lot more visitors.
issue: The kids Midway area has not been upgraded and feels outdated compared to its past condition
fixes: Launch a **digital concept‑capture sprint** using Miro boards to map current attractions, identify low‑ROI items, and prototype new interactive stations; set a two‑week timeline with weekly sprints and assign a product manager to lead. | - Negotiate a phased **capital‑upgrade contract** (e.g., $150k in FY24) with a local amusement‑equipment vendor, specifying quarterly milestone deliveries tracked via a shared Google Sheet that logs installation dates and budget variances. | - Deploy **QR‑code feedback kiosks** at the entrance and midway that feed real‑time NPS scores into a Power BI dashboard, triggering alerts when satisfaction falls below 70% so corrective actions can be taken within 48 hours. | - Introduce a **monthly “Kids Midway Refresh” rotation** managed through Asana, assigning a rotating team of three staff members to replace one static attraction with a themed pop‑up game; measure success by tracking attendance spikes >10 % during refresh periods.
rationale: All recommendations are highly relevant to the outdated Kids Midway issue, explicitly actionable with clear steps, and grounded in specific tools (Miro, Asana, Power BI, Google Sheets). However, feasibility is severely compromised: the $150k capital upgrade, phased delivery, and new QR-code kiosks require significant budget, vendor negotiation, and infrastructure changes—far beyond trivial or low effort. This crosses into high-effort territory, justifying a 2 on feasibility.
